This whole PR looks great, except for this part where there are these two undocumented functions.  They also behave in a somewhat opposite way compared to the others, which makes the class interface as a whole not cohesive and hard to understand.  I get what it's doing, but interface complexity is one of the roots of software bugs, and so should be avoided.

Really the interface for this class should either require some kind of setup before use for all the functions uniformly, or not require setup at all, as it previously did.  Not a mixture of both, and especially not one that isn't documented in any way.  

So what about this.  There can be a setup that takes only the 4 stride and padding arguments.  Calling that function will be required before any other function is executed.  Then the rest of the functions can do an internal allocation, initialization, or whatever, if they need it, to make everything work.  This would mean removing the other operator() that takes stride and padding.  It seems like that would cover everything you want to do but provide for a uniform and documentable interface.  Does that sound right or am I missing something?

The string `CurrentUserDefault()` does not represent a valid user ID, so cannot be a "default value" for the owner field which is a PrimaryKeyRelatedField.

As far as I know, the OpenAPI specification provides no way to specify a default based on external state such as currently authenticated user, so this can't really be expressed by spec. The best you could hope for would be to document the behavior in a description, e.g. "if `owner` is not provided, it will be assumed to be the currently logged in user".

Since this project aims for spec compliance and codegen tool compatibility, we cannot just use arbitrary strings just for documentation purposes in the `default` field.

> The number of bytes read or written must always be an integer multiple of the number of channels multiplied by the number of bytes required to represent one sample from one channel.

Personally, I would drop this section, and simply leave the example at `transaction.manager.run(do_something)`. 

It's fine if `run` can be used as a decorator for those that want to, but I don't feel like it's something we should encourage. That's so rarely done, in comparison to how decorators are normally used, that it is unexpected and surprising; most people won't be expecting the instant execution and will be confused upon reading the code. 

I know that came up on the mailing list, but even "run" to me doesn't sufficiently imply the concept of "no, really, this is not a normal decorator, this is happening now."

Another thing I dislike about the use of decorators to actually run a function is that (once you understand that's what's happening) you have to read the function body, and *then* go back up and remember that it's actually being done now. It's backwards (or upside down?) and less natural to read than definition followed by execution.

It has the disadvantage of not naturally returning a result, too. Or rather, yes, it does return a result, but the result isn't the function as one would expect from the `def` statement. This, to me, is very surprising code:

```python
@transaction.manager.run
def create_user():
   ...
   return some_user if user_created else None

if create_user is not None: 
   # What? How could it be None, we just def'd it
```

If you try to use this pattern, you run into a conflict: naming the function such that it's meaningful in the transaction note, and naming the function such that it describes what it's actually doing, conflicts with naming the function such that it makes a good result variable name. Wouldn't this be more clear:
```python
def create_user():
   ...
   return some_user if user_created else None

user = transaction.manager.run(create_user)
if user is not None:
   ...
```

Finally, as a minor point, if `run` *isn't* usable as a decorator, it may make it somewhat easier to support passing arguments to the function, which in turn supports defining functions only once (not at execution time), which can improve code sharing, decrease execution time and reduce garbage collection (inner functions tend to lead to cycles unless you're extremely careful).

```python
user = transaction.manager.run(create_user, username='foo')
```

In fact, the numpy implementation for trig acting on objects looks for the corresponding method and calls that.  So `numpy.cos(theta)` just calls `theta.cos()` behind the scenes. There are three cases in this test: 1) A gauge with an explicit end time, 2) a cumulative metric, and 3) a gauge with an implicit end time. As the server sends chunks of a row, it only sends the row key/column family/column qualifier for a new cell if it is different than the last cell (as an optimization). Yes, that's expected. Failing prop validation doesn't prevent the component from trying to render, it only provides warnings to the developer in vue's dev tools.

Your two options here are:

1. figure out why the parent or tests are passing `null` in as a value for `contents` and stop that from happening
2. keep the default value as `[]` and remove `required: true`

(1) seems more correct, but (2) is acceptable as a quick hack to just get things working and move forward

The -1 thing is also baked into the `_translate_socket_errors_to_stream_errors`... on Unix, it's whole job is to convert EBADF into a `ClosedStreamError` (because EBADF is what you get when you try to use -1 as an fd), and convert everything else into `BrokenStreamError`.

It might make sense to write a `_translate_pipe_errors_to_stream_errors`, even if it's very similar to the socket version, just because both error-converters are so tightly coupled to internal implementation decisions that it's nice to not have to think about code in other files when looking at them.
